findings.section: Before You Start Building: The Credential Setup (D9)
findings.issue: The document states "Do this first. This week. Before anything else" and lists 12 accounts, but does not explain what credentials are or why a non-technical founder needs to understand them.
findings.evidence: Section header and opening paragraph assume founder understands what "accounts," "credentials," and ".env.local" mean. No plain-language explanation provided.
findings.recommendation: Add a brief plain-language explanation: "These are login credentials for online services. Think of them as usernames and passwords that your application will use to send emails, process payments, and store data. The .env.local file is a secure vault where these credentials live on your server—not in your code, so they're never accidentally exposed."

findings.section: The Build Process: Your Roadmap → How Build Cards Work
findings.issue: The example build card (FC-003) uses technical terminology without founder-level explanation: "Clerk JWT token," "PaymentIntent," "junction rows," "API failure."
findings.evidence: Example card includes: "Validate Clerk JWT token," "create booking_courses junction rows," "Stripe API to create PaymentIntent," "Stripe API failure returns 500"—all explained at developer level, not founder level.
findings.recommendation: Add a short translator paragraph before the example: "Build cards look technical because they're instructions for developers or AI tools. You don't need to understand every detail—focus on the 'What to build' section and 'Success criteria.' If a card references 'JWT tokens' or 'API calls,' that just means: 'the system securely identifies the logged-in user' and 'the system talks to another service.' The cards are precise so builders can execute without ambiguity, not so founders must comprehend every line."

findings.issue: The technology stack section explains each choice with "Why," but does not connect any choice to Ocean Golf's specific business model or constraints (luxury golf trips, multi-stakeholder coordination, payment processing).
findings.evidence: Stack explanation is generic: "React (Next.js 15) — Modern, component-based, great dev experience." Does not explain why these choices serve Ocean Golf specifically (e.g., "PostgreSQL chosen because golf course booking data has complex relationships—one trip can include multiple courses, multiple payments, multiple confirmations—that PostgreSQL handles elegantly").
findings.recommendation: Add one sentence per major technology that connects to Ocean Golf's business context: "PostgreSQL — Golf trips involve coordinated multi-course bookings. PostgreSQL's relational database design ensures that courses, bookings, and payments stay synchronized." "Stripe — Golf trip payments are high-value and international (US travel agents to Mexican courses). Stripe handles multi-currency pricing and refunds seamlessly." "Clerk authentication — Travel agents often work with enterprise single sign-on. Clerk supports this now, so you can add enterprise features later without rebuilding auth."

findings.issue: The timeline is precise for build phases, but does not explain the business meaning of each gate: why does Week 1–2 require "course database seeded with 14 courses" specifically, and what does failure mean for the overall launch?
findings.evidence: Gate pass criteria: "Course database created, booking form creates booking records" — factually clear, but founder cannot assess whether this is on track without understanding why it matters. What breaks if this is delayed one week? What is the dependency downstream?
findings.recommendation: For each gate, add one sentence explaining the business consequence: "Week 1–2 gate: Course database + working booking form. Why: If this gate fails, Lucia cannot start testing the coordination flow in Week 3. This cascades: if coordination is delayed, your June 10 launch date slips, which delays pre-launch setup and pushes launch past September 1."

findings.section: Critical Success Criteria for Phase 7 Build
findings.issue: The success criteria list 12 items marked with checkboxes, but does not explain what happens if any single item is NOT checked by June 10.
findings.evidence: Criteria are listed as "Zero critical bugs," "All 120 build cards complete," etc., with a footer statement "Failure to meet any of these means Phase 9 is delayed." But founder may not understand: What IS Phase 9? How many days of delay? What is the financial impact?
findings.recommendation: Add a plain-language consequence statement: "If any of these criteria is not met by June 10, Phase 9 (pre-launch setup and beta testing) is delayed. Each week of delay compresses your pre-launch testing window, which increases the risk of bugs reaching production. Worst case: missing June 10 pushes your September 1 launch date, which impacts your Q3 revenue projections in D10."

findings.section: Where to Get Help → If You Have Questions About the Specification
findings.issue: The help section references D2, D4, D6, D20 by deliverable name, but does not explain how a founder FINDS these documents in the repository or identifies which document to open for a given question type.
findings.evidence: Example: "D2 for authorization questions. Who can do what? It's in there." Founder must know that D2 is "User Roles & Authorization" and be able to navigate the repository to find it. No navigation instruction provided.
findings.recommendation: Add navigation guidance: "Look in the `/operations` folder for D2. If you're unsure which deliverable to check, use the 'What's In This Package' section above—it lists what each document covers. Or search for a keyword (e.g., 'Lucia,' 'permission,' 'course') and the deliverable name will appear nearby."

findings.issue: The tools section mentions "Cursor IDE," "Claude Code," and "GitHub Spec Kit," but does not explain what these tools ARE or whether the founder needs to SET THEM UP before starting.
findings.evidence: Text reads: "Cursor IDE: For individual cards... Claude Code: For multi-file features... GitHub Spec Kit: Optional." No explanation of what these tools do, where to download them, or cost. Founder may assume they come with the specification package.
findings.recommendation: Add a brief tool orientation: "These are three different approaches to executing build cards. Cursor IDE is a code editor you install locally (free). Claude Code is an AI-powered tool inside Claude.ai (free with Claude subscription). GitHub Spec Kit is an optional visualization tool (free). All three read the same build card format. You don't need all three—pick one that matches your workflow. D9 will tell you how to set up the one you choose."

findings.issue: The checklist is comprehensive, but the final two items assume an assigned "lead developer" and weekly syncs with Lucia, which may not apply if Rafael (the founder) is solo-building.
findings.evidence: Checklist includes: "Assigned a lead developer or AI tool responsible for each week's build cards" and "Scheduled weekly syncs with Lucia." These assume a team structure. Rafael may be building alone with an AI tool.
findings.recommendation: Expand the language to cover solo-build scenarios: "Assigned a lead developer OR designated which AI tool (Cursor/Claude Code) will handle each week's build cards" and "Scheduled weekly syncs with Lucia (if she's available) OR plan solo testing checkpoints where you verify each week's output against success criteria."

findings.section: WELCOME TO YOUR OCEAN GOLF SPECIFICATION PACKAGE
findings.issue: The opening paragraph uses the phrase "Nothing is left to guesswork," which sets an expectation of 100% specification completeness that may not hold for emergent issues discovered during build.
findings.evidence: Opening states: "Nothing is left to guesswork. No 'figure it out as you go.' Every feature, every database table, every API endpoint... is documented."
findings.recommendation: Soften to: "Everything essential is specified. No ambiguity on the features you're building, the database structure, or the API design. That said, once you start building, you may discover edge cases or technical issues the spec didn't anticipate. When that happens, use the decision-making framework in D20 to resolve it quickly without derailing the schedule."
